tweets.text: Well there's a reason why we said this isn't an "AI bearish index" or for that matter "AI bullish index". We merely said that it's not necessarily bearish, esp in the long run, that token index continues to travel down, which is also the point @Conor_Coops made. If we transition from a state, in which two closed models dominate and are the the source of most compute demand, towards a state, in which many enterprises rent/own compute and host own post-trained open models, the total compute market could indeed become bigger if not substantially bigger. That is a question of demand elasticity and ultimate ROI from wider AI adoption into workflows. I think it's possible to hold two thoughts in one's brain at the same time?

tweets.text:
TVL is the laziest way to evaluate a protocol.

That becomes clear when you compare @LidoFinance and @jito_sol.

Jito ($786M) vs Lido ($17.7B).
Lido is 22x larger by TVL.

But their annual fees?

→ Lido: $740M
→ Jito: $259M

Lido mostly earns fees from staking rewards on deposited ETH.

Jito earns fees from staking + MEV + priority fees through its block engine on Solana.

Most of Jito's fees come from MEV and priority fees across Solana's blockspace, so it earns more per dollar deposited.

Capital size ≠ capital efficiency.

tweets.text:
Grabbed some $EWY at 155.52, $AAOI at $93, $NBIS at 166.3, $SNDK at 1395. AAOI will be a longer term position for me, same with NBIS. Ewy and SNDK ill dump within the week most likely. 

The one thing I would caution however is there's a very real possibility that we have some extended chop/sideways period due to the amount of deleveraging that occurred. I think it would be quite easy to get harmed each direction. At least until vol dies off a bit. These entries were the result of waiting 6 weeks. The market structure is pretty poor now overall, so I'd rather keep the higher conviction stuff, and start building those positions. As we could easily bleed lower. Will go into more of my detailed thinking tomorrow when I have some time. But the 6 weeks of waiting for these looks to be off to a great start each direction

tweets.text:
From an ecological perspective, western frontier models are a common pool resource that Chinese open weight models exploit. They are finite, because if you exploit them too hard, they lose the incentive and resources to train new models, and everyone loses. (Chinese models aren’t magically good, they are good because Ant and OAI spend a lot of money on training). 

If you think about it like a parasite/host relationship, Chinese models are a parasite that needs to calibrate its exploitation so that the host doesn’t die. Kimi arguably made a mistake. It overexploited the host and imperiled the Ant OAI growth story. So at equilibrium you’d expect open weight models to “tastefully” exploit the western frontier and choose to remain 6 months behind and slightly inferior capability wise.

However perhaps Kimi, Qwen, GLM, deepseek etc may not be playing an equilibrium game. They might be under orders to puncture the American lab growth story, even if it means killing their own host. Maybe the time is now because OAI and Ant are most vulnerable ahead of the IPO. Maybe Xi just had a bad day. Or maybe Kimi defected from the gentleman’s agreement that the Chinese labs share to not kill the host that they all rely on.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: China is now leading the AI arms race after spending a tenth what the US did https://t.co/sJOwU0KZhp

thread_tweets.text:
I think the answer here is that China is happy to “liberate” American frontier capabilities via open weight distilled models for a soft power win globally. By bringing everyone up to the frontier China destroys an asymmetry that benefited the US alone. China doesn’t uniquely benefit from this but it doesn’t cost them very much and it’s destroys a couple trillion $ worth of shareholder capital and one of the hallmarks of American exceptionalism. 

The over exploitation of Ant/OAI and lack of a viable business model for any of the Chinese labs demonstrates this. There’s no long term sustainable equilibrium sought and no real economic model. 

Of course, America still has an advantage in AI even if OAI and Ant are hamstrung/commoditized, because the quantity of compute still matters, even if the whole world runs open weight models. So the AI capex spend is not for nothing - you still get a monstrous amount of compute!

tweets.text: Stepped out of a wedding to record this pod. Really enjoyed it. Going into it, I wanted to make sure the points I conveyed were positive and optimistic because negativity is overrated. I think you always want to be optimistic, and sometimes being too realistic gets us in this trap of not appreciating how wonderful it is to be alive at this moment in time. And that’s all you need to know to carry TF on

tweets.text:
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗕𝗮𝗰𝗸𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀 

Somewhere at the start of my career in New York City, at a firm called First New York Securities, the trader I worked for gave me my first assignment: backtesting trading indicators.

It was a great lesson, but probably not the one he intended.

The biggest mistake investors make is assuming that because an indicator worked in the past, it will continue to work in the future.

Markets evolve.

The participants change. The algorithms change. Passive investing changes market flows. Leveraged ETFs amplify short-term price movements. Even the S&P 500 itself has changed. Today, the top 10 companies represent roughly 40% of the index, and most of that concentration is in AI-related or AI-enabling businesses.

This is not the same market we were backtesting 5 or 20 years ago.

Backtesting tells you how a strategy would have performed under yesterday’s conditions. It says very little about whether those conditions still exist.

That’s why I don’t build my investment process around indicators or historical backtests. I focus on understanding how today’s market is different from yesterday’s.

Because in markets, adaptation beats memorization every time.

@zenbynature91 Bobby, nobody knows the future. Don’t waste your time trying to predict it.
My answer would be just as good as my nephew’s.

Focus on managing risk and adapting to new information, not on pretending you know what’s coming next.

tweets.text:
Existence itself is a miracle - the rest is science. 

There is no single meaning to existence - if there were, we’d be slaves to it.

Within existence, there are rules of logic and science. If it was magic, the world would be un-navigable.

Truly the best of all possible worlds.

tweets.text:
Your decision should be to stop hitting the ATM at $100 (only ATM at your own discretion anywhere above $100) and to stop trying to target a $99-$100 range because it's literally impossible. Best range you can get is probably $95-$105

The STRC trilemma is that you can't have these 3 things at the same time:

- high market cap for STRC (ie scale)
- dividend rate that only adjusts once a month and by 50bps max (ie quasi stable on short timeframes)
- a tight 1% price peg

Your decision should be to stop hitting the ATM at $100 (only ATM at your own discretion anywhere above $100) and to stop trying to target a $99-$100 range because it's literally impossible. Best range you can get is probably $95-$105

The STRC trilemma is that you can't have these 3 things at the same time:

- high market cap for STRC (ie scale)
- dividend rate that only adjusts once a month and by 50bps max (ie quasi stable on short timeframes)
- a tight 1% price peg
